title: Codon Redundancy
summary: Most amino acids are encoded by more than one codon — there are 64 codons but only 20 amino acids. The redundancy is concentrated in the 3rd codon position (the "wobble" position), where multiple bases often code for the same amino acid. This is why some single-base substitutions are silent (no protein change) — the codon still codes for the same amino acid.
